work-dev external research | NOT Record truth
Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CDClFY-R0dI | video_id: CDClFY-R0dI | Nate B Jones / AI News & Strategy Daily
Title: A Markdown File Just Replaced Your Most Expensive Design Meeting. (Google Stitch)
Transcript: operator-supplied (ASR-style text).

---

The world is getting overloaded in AI slump. But what makes it useful? When does the creative process assisted by AI become something we actually care about? I think three big releases dropped in the last week or so that we need to pay attention to as builders. This is not about taking jobs away from designers.
It's actually about rethinking how we do design in the age of AI. I want to get into detail, talk about each release, talk about what it does for you, and talk about where we can go with it to build appropriately. First, Google updated Stitch. It's a free tool where you describe an app in words and it draws the screens for you.
 It's not wireframes. It's actually a finished looking UI with multiple pages at once and you can talk about it out loud and it's going to design it in real time. Yes, you can actually do voice to design now. It's connected to coding tools so you don't have to ask yourself whether this is buildable.
 It's actually just going to read the design and build against it appropriately. And there isn't going to be any more exporting to Figma. And that is why Figma stock tanked because fundamentally when you have based your entire value proposition around this era idea that all of our job families are separate, you get into trouble in the 20 era world where job roles are blurring and we're all moving toward the command line.
 Second big release to pay attention to is Remotion. It's a video framework that's been growing quietly since January. that's crossed over 150,000 installs as a claude code skill. And all you have to do to use Remotion is just to describe a video like, "Hey, make a 30-se secondond product demo of the product we've been building together.
 Please show these three features and show a call to action." And the agent will write the code that renders it into an actual MP4 video. The third one is Blender MCP and it takes a bit of a different approach. It's at about 17,000 GitHub stars. And what Blender does is it actually builds a 3D scene in front of you.
 So you can do something like say, "Hey, I want to create a beach scene for my product and this is what I want it to look like." And Blender will build the three-dimensional version for you right in front of you. So people who've never touched Blender are doing things like generating uh room walkthroughs or architectural assets for their uh general contractors.
 There are people who are using Blender for what it was originally designed for, which is video game graphics. But they're creating immersive walkthrough generative worlds. And so, we're just at the beginning of figuring out what all this looks like. But I think we know enough now to start to talk about the idea that design is shifting to the command line and talk about the implications of that.
 But first, let's talk through each of these pipelines and understand where we're going. The one commonality we have across all three is that they all depend on the universal standard for AI connectors, the MCP. In other words, we are seeing again and again over and over that MCP is becoming the USB plug for AI where you can just take a tool and say I want to make it available at the command line.
 It becomes an MCP server and you can just begin to work with it. That's how Remotion went from a little website that people might go to and do stuff on to a fast growing GitHub community. They made it an MCP and they made it available to people to use as a skill inside the terminal. That is becoming the growth hack of 2026. If you have a product, ask yourself, why isn't it an MCP? It should be an MCP.
 If it's not an MCP, you got problems. So, why does the command line matter so much? I think this is a much bigger deal than the tool and the 2026 use case. I think most of the time we like to talk about these products as if they're big flashy releases and and we miss some of the larger changes that are happening. So what I am seeing is that the 2010s era triangle of product and engineering and design is starting to blur together.
We've talked about that. But the thing we haven't talked about that I've seen discussed only rarely because I think it's a nuance topic that tends to sort of run away into the job loss debate. We don't talk about the fact that the product design engineering triangle often did not work well in practice. And I say that as someone who lived it, right? And I would say of the product design engineering triangles I was a part of, the vast majority were not seamless.
 We had issues where engineering was the break and said you couldn't build this. We had issues where design was slow and said I need some more time to push pixels. We had issues where product namely me liked to go fast and buy us for action and everyone else was worried about that. But the point is this.
 In all of those cases, we were gated by the ability to sequentially get pixels on the page and then later find out if it was buildable. Best circumstances I found, the best teamwork angles I found in that 2010 world was when you could colllocate and it was the 2010s ideal, right? The designer would sit with the engineer with product. We would all huddle around the screen and we would mess with stuff together and we would see what worked.
 The beauty of that is even though that was rare in the 2010s, this kind of tool with the command line is making that more possible for everybody today. So in the past, you had to depend on the right people being in the room and having some degree of frankly role blurring even though we didn't talk about it that way.
The designer needed to understand a bit of how JavaScript worked. the the engineer needed to understand a little bit about how you put polish on the front end and product needed to be able to sit there and sort of put the two together and look at the offer and the customer. Well, now at the command line, you can actually invoke something that is by definition buildable because that's how this works.
 If it's not buildable, cloud code can't touch it. And also, you can start to bring in multiple options for design iteration very quickly. And that was always one of the things we struggled with. It's the idea that design takes time. Designing something in a polished way takes more time. If you want to get through a variety of iterations, design is something that you can do from the command line now and quickly get through them.
 Now, I am here to tell you if you want extraordinary polish, you will still need to have a designer hand finish this stuff. But so often right now, what the designer needs to do is just put together from a design library the next release. Like if you wonder how are designers at Enthropic doing this? How are designers at OpenAI doing this? They are in the code.
 They are working from the command line. They are working to code something up and they're working from the library of components they have and they're putting it together in a way that's very rapid so they don't slow the team down. That is command line design thinking and it applies to the video space as much as it applies to creative.
So I don't see highass designers leaving the room here. I think the AI replaces designers narrative is cheap and it's distracting. I see highclass designers and really I've talked to them sitting at the command line. I see them sitting with engineering and product pushing through ideas into code in real time.
And instead of the whole table wondering whether the design is buildable, it's just right there. It's prototyped. It's exportable. It goes right to the codebase. I'm really bullish on this and it's not because it replaces designers. It's because it abstracts away the part of design that was never the point.
 The point was always the experience for the customer, the feeling, the flow, the moment when a user thinks this is what I needed. That judgment, you can't automate that away. It gets amplified because now the person who has it can iterate at the speed of language instead of at the speed of Figma. What shift in the last few weeks is the beginning of that shift.
 And it changes the cost structure of creative work in the same way vibe coding changed the cost structure of software. So let's walk through these pipelines. Let's see what we can actually ship, what works, what's rough around the edges, and what the workflow looks like when you start to train it together. And if you're not a designer, this is even more relevant to you because design has always been a short commodity.
 It's been hard to get design expertise. People often do without. And now you can get maybe not an excellent human designer quality, but at least more designer quality than you could get without design in the room, which is great. So, first, Stitch. Google originally launched Stitch at IO 2025 as a very quiet labs experiment. It was a texttoi generator running on Gemini producing one screen at a time that looked useful but frankly I thought limited and a lot of people thought limited.
 It's the kind of thing you see in the demo and you sort of forget about the March update is not that Google redesigned the entire product around what they're calling vibe design. the design equivalent of vibe coding. Instead of opening a blank canvas and placing your components, all you do is you describe your business objective, a user feeling, or a product concept in natural language.
 Stitch will then generate multiple highfidelity UI directions simultaneously, and it will pull together up to five screens at once on an infinite canvas where you can then drop in images and text and code snippets and competitor screenshots as well. Now, this was the killer app. And it turns out that this is a nice parable for how difficult it is for even large companies to find product market fit today. They debuted this.
 Google debuted this back in IO. Figma shares didn't really move. It took actually getting the product right, moving it to the command line, making it simple to use, expanding the blast radius of what you can do with design. Now, Figma shares are responding in the markets because now the now the stock market, the the Wall Street guys who are not designers see the obvious connection to Figma.
 So, here's what Stitch actually does. You can open a canvas. You can just talk to the canvas because there's voice mode and you just tell it what you want, right? If this isn't specific enough for you, let me give you a prompt, right? Say, "Design a landing page for an AI writing assistant.
" The hero section has a headline and CTA. There's feature sections with benefit cards. Please put pricing section. make it two tiers and Stitch will just generate complete designs, not wireframes, by the way. Highfidelity screens with real typography, real color pallets, good spacing, good component hierarchy, and it gets more interesting from there.
 The design agent doesn't just generate and forget. It actually holds the entire project context, all the screens, all the decisions, every piece of reference material you've dropped onto the canvas, and it reasons across all of it when you ask for changes or edits. So if you want three different navigation layouts, you can ask and it will see the original and it will know how to adjust.
 If you want to see the whole app in a dark pallet, just say so. The agent will track the project's evolution the way a senior designer tracks a design system holistically, not just screen by screen. And the agent manager does more than that. It actually lets you branch and compare design directions, which is one of the things I'm most excited about in the age of AI because we were always gated in our optionality by the time it took to make designs. Not anymore.
 You can now version control. You can do creative exploration. You can pursue five different approaches simultaneously and evaluate them side by side and merge the pieces you like. And you get instant prototyping that converts any static design into a clickable flow. Just hit play and you can walk the user journey.
So, Stitch even autogenerates the logical next screen based on user interactions. Talk about generative UI, right? And it predicts what comes after a button press by reasoning about the product structure and the way you've talked about it. And this is what really killed Figma. It's free. It's free. It gives you 350 generations a month for nothing. 0 cents.
 And that's what you get when you're Google and all of AI is assigned that to you. But the feature that's most interesting and frankly that most coverage missed. I'm not sure why is the design. Markdown file. We all know markdown files are critical in AI. What design domarkdown as an export from Stitch gives you is a full agent readable file that captures a project's design system as you've evolved it in Stitch.
 So you not only export the code but you get a durable record of colors of typography of spacing rules of component patterns and you can extract them from any URL that you want. So you can go and say, "Hey, have a look at this URL. Please pull the design markdown." And you can get a sense of what that site that you admire actually looks like from a designer's perspective.
 Now, people who aren't smart are going to rip this off and say, "Okay, I'm going to take the site that I like and copy it." People who are smart will use it as inspiration and actually push it farther because they have a sense of where they want to go. Again, clarity of intent is a differentiator in the age of AI.
 And Google isn't locking this into their ecosystem. As I said at the top, this is MCP readable, which means you can grab it from Cloud Code, you can grab it from Chad GPT, and yes, you can grab it from Google's own anti-gravity tool. Your coding agent that you prefer can read your design system while it builds. Google also shipped official Claude code skills, which is a sign of how dominant Claude is when Google has to ship claude code skills with their launch.
 But they shipped installable playbooks that teach the agent how to work with stitch outputs. The pipeline works right now, right? You can design the business objective today. Stitch will generate the UI. The coding agent will read the design.mmarkdown file and just start building. There's no Figma export. There's no handoff document.
 There's no the developer got the design document wrong, which I've heard a lot. It simplifies the number of handoffs in the system, which is one of the big things we're seeing as a trend in AI. You start to delete these handoffs as agents get more capable. That's what we're seeing that Google did here.
 Now, here's the rough part. I want to be honest with you. The design quality is not yet at a point where most senior designers would sign off on it and say, "This is great. I want to show it as is to a client, but it's better than a prototype, and this is the dumbest and worst it will ever be.
" And so, I would look at it as a is it a way to get an MVP into market if I can't work with a designer? B, is it a way for a designer to work faster and be able to polish more effectively because we've tested a bunch of different directions? and then see if we can make it this good without pre-built components. Can we start to bring our own design system into play and bring extra polish to the process? And so there's a lot of directions you can go with this, but I want to be clear that you should not view this the way I think probably Google markets it as a magic
designer in a box. View it more as a magic junior designer in a box that does a faster, better job at prototyping, but isn't going to be perfect, especially if you're not clear on intent. And by the way, that is valuable, especially if it's free. The phase that we're talking about, design exploration, MVP development, that used to cost thousands of dollars just to get that part before you got to the polishing. Now it's free.
This is what we mean when we say the cost of software is falling to zero. Remotion number two, now we're going from design to video. Video is becoming code. So, in January 20th, Breeotion, which is a React framework that treats video as code, I know it's a head scratcher, but but stay with me, launched an agent skill for cloud code.
Within 48 hours, the demo video they made had over 6 million views. And 8 weeks later, the skill sits at 150,000 installs or more on skills.sh, which is an open directory for AI agent skills. It's the number one skill that is not made by a company named Versel, Anthropic, or Microsoft. And it's what it does is really simple, right? You just install the reotion skill into Claude code with a single terminal command.
 Then you describe the video in plain English and Claude writes the React components, the code components that define every single frame of the video. If that sounds exhausting to you, just be glad Claude is doing it. It defines the text animations. It defines the motion graphics. It defines the data visualizations. It defines the captions.
It defines the transitions. Remotion renders all of those components into a video file. MP4. The whole loop runs locally on your machine for free other than the Claude Code subscription. So, you're not paying extra for that. I want to be really clear here because there's a ton of misunderstanding.
 This is not the same thing as AI generated video. Say it after me. It's not the same thing. Tools like Sora and Runway generate pixels from prompts and they can be impressive but also inconsistent. They're not editable easily. They can be expensive to iterate on. Remotion generates code that renders video. So every element is a React component that you can modify, version control, and parameterize.
 If you change one variable and rerender 100 localized versions, that's fine. That's free other than your cloud code subscription. If you update the data source and every chart in the video updates automatically, again, it's fine. So this is programmable video, which is in some ways more powerful than generative video because it's easier to edit and adjust.
 And that distinction matters enormously for any of us who are producing video content at scale. Remotion collapses the video production last mile. The person who knows what the video should communicate just says what it should say directly. The agent handles the timeline. And because the output is code, not a locked export, anyone on the team can modify it without re-entering the editing tool.
 And you can do a lot if it's just code. You might think, "Oh, it's going to look terrible." But one creator, Sabrina.dev, dev documented her complete pipeline. She was able to generate a promotional video from one prompt, having Claude browse the web to take real screenshots of GitHub repositories for inclusion in the video, and then added in her headshot and added background music from follow-up prompts.
 And the whole workflow was the command line. No Premiere, no After Effects, no timeline editor. She just got it done. I want you to again look at the falling cost of compute and the value of design here. This is freeing our imaginations. That's the through line here for through these first two releases through Stitch and through Remotion.
 We can imagine and therefore we can create cheaply. Before if you wanted to make a polished clip on a MacBook Pro, it's it's heavy CPU usage. It takes a while. It's not easy to edit. Sometimes it will take an hour to render, two hours to render. And complex animations can break down fast. Now, I want to be honest with you.
 If you're doing extremely complex animations in clawed code to reotion, you also have issues because if you have multiple overlapping elements or fancy transitions or very intricate timing, you may not get a perfect render. The sweet spot right now seems to be very clean motion graphics like ask it for text animations, data visualizations, product demos, terminal recordings, which honestly is a ton of product space to operate in, and it's going to do very well.
 So, think of this as a shortorthhand for the kind of content that drives so much of a digital business like product demos, feature announcements, data stories, social clips. All of those can be done from the command line in Remotion. And there's no incremental cost for you because you're working with claude code. And effectively, Remotion is giving you the skill that Claude Code needs to write code that generates that video.
 And so, the gating factor on your video is the quality of your input. And if you're wondering, did I put something together on Substack to make it easier to prompt remotion? I 100% did. There is a guide for this. And uh your mileage may vary, but it sure is better if you're using some structured inputs.
 And so we we'll we'll get into that. You can dive into the substack. All right. Number three, also a creative tool released in just the last few weeks, Blender MCP, which is really three-dimensional gets a chat window. Blender is, think of it as like a nuclear reactor for creative tools, right? You get professionalgrade 3D modeling. You get animation.
 You get rendering. You get simulation all in one thing. And it's used in feature films. It's used in game studios. It's used in architectural visualization. It is by universal consensus one of the most complex pieces of software a human can attempt to learn. The interface has roughly 1500 operators. I'm not kidding.
A Python API that exposes almost every internal function and a learning curve that is measured for some people in years. Now, Blender MCP simplifies all that. All you do is you type a natural language description like create a beach scene with palm trees and sunset lighting and you watch the 3D environment assemble itself in real time.
 In other words, by the power of MCP because the function of MCP is to enable an LLM to creatively manipulate tool sets. Claude manipulates the tool sets of Blender for you without that learning curve. And so the beach scene assembles itself. Objects appear, materials get applied, lighting adjusts, and all of it is controlled and edited by AI writing and executing against Blender's Python API through a socket-based bridge.
 The repo is obviously popular, right? It has over 17,000 GitHub stars, 1500 plus forks as people build off of it. It integrates with uh a tool called Polyhaven for free high-quality assets like HDRIs and textures and 3D models, and with SketchFab if you want an even wider library. You can also connect it to Hyper 3D for text to 3D model generation.
 Meaning you can describe a character or object, have it generated and then drop it into your scene without leaving the conversation. I can describe a character like an open claw crab and drop it into the beach scene without leaving the conversation. The community response has predictably been massive. Users have described it as a gamecher and I think they're underselling it.
When you have deterministic software that is as complex as Blender and there are other cases like that, MCP plus the command line is a massive simplifier. Which is why I like to say over and over again, don't be afraid of the terminal. It's actually simpler than most of your other options out there because you can now describe in natural language.
 This is where the camera should be. This is what the setting should be. This is what I want to appear for a three-dimensional scene. And instead of spending months or years learning Blender, you just have it made for you in a few seconds and you're done and you can edit at any time. And if you think that's silly, I don't need that.
 Then your job probably isn't architecture previsualization or gaming prototyping or frankly product mockups or any workflow where the bottleneck is getting from concept to proof of concept very fast. There are a lot of cases where we have three-dimensional visualization needs and it has been difficult to get to that level of fidelity quickly.
 Again, we see a collapse in the cost of creativity here. It's not that you're not going to be creative. It's that you're more free to be creative because it just is faster to get from idea to voice to the thing is there. It's as close as the breath now. And there's a larger thing here that I've been thinking about when we were working through the classic product triangle in the 2010s where you had engineer product design together.
 So much of what we wrestled with was context loss. So much of what made those dynamics work when they worked was all of us together at one computer talking when there was zero context loss. You have zero context loss in all of these three tools because it's so easy to go from just describing what you want to getting something back and getting something visual.
 And I want to call out that these are primitives, right? You can think of these visuals as components in your workflow. And one of the big skills of 2026 is taking these individual components and smartly integrating them into a larger workflow. Let me give you a specific example here. This one's hot off the presses. On March the 20th, Noah's Way announced cloud-based scheduling task for cloud code.
 Right? You set a repo, you schedule it, and you prompt. Claude then runs on a cadence in its cloud infrastructure and your laptop can still be closed and your terminal can be off and the work still gets done. Now the example he posted was developer facing, right? A you can schedule a daily job. It reviews all the PR shipped since yesterday and updates the docs.
 That's fine. That's useful. But think about it just in the context of what we've been talking about today. Creative pipelines. You can schedule a weekly job that pulls all of the shipping that you've done for your product this week and generates a Remotion video for customers that summarizes the key points with motion graphics and renders it in aspect ratios and cues it up ready to upload so you can have an attractive launch video for customers every single week.
 You can schedule a daily job that checks your analytics dashboard and it will generate a data visualization video showing yesterday's metrics and it will send it to the team Slack channel. You can schedule a job that monitors your product's change log and generates updated UI screenshots via Stitch and rebuilds the marketing site's feature gallery every time a new version drops.
Which you think that one's not worth it? I got to tell you, the number of conversations that I have had with marketing and documentation teams as product just to explain this thing launched, you got to remember to update the site. We need to update the docs. Is everything up to date? These kinds of things are now trivial.
 If you hook up your creative primitives to your scheduling primitives and that's what I want you to start to think in terms of these are Lego building blocks for workflows that you care about and what Gates now is you. What do you care about? What are you passionate about? How does your workflow work well when you can have the power of these tools at the command line? Because the entire pipeline from idea to artifact, from idea to creative is now describable in English, executed on by agents for you, and scheduled to repeat autonomously.
And I keep emphasizing this is not expensive. You can do the remotion skill for the cost of claude code. Google's giving you free iterations on Stitch. It's cheap now. And look, I've been watching creative tools get disrupted for 20 years now. Photoshop by Canva, After Effects by Simpler Motion Tools, Sketch by Figma, Figma by AI.
 The pattern is always the same. A new tool lowers the floor of who can produce good enough work and incumbents start to panic and then reality sets in. The floor dropped, but the ceiling didn't move. In other words, more of us got in the door as creatives, but excellence still matters.
 The difference now is how deep the floor dropped. Like if we're talking about the depth of change that this represents when it's basically free to generate designs, the floor dropped into the basement because anybody can now generate designs. And the distance between good enough and extraordinarily excellent is now something that we have to talk about as a different skill for designers because before so much of design was can you execute the thing.
Now so much of the design is can I polish the thing? Can I look at the thing and say, "This is what's not good enough specifically, and this is the edit I want to make. And can I do it quickly and consistently and apply that skill and potentially even teach that skill so others can learn it? Can I make the thing I know to be right or wrong, my sniff test, something that I can act on very fast as I look through all of these designs so that I am not the bottleneck for AI and the design creativity we can now have." And so I
think the thing to take away is the people who win here are not the people who individually master these tools. That's sort of like being the person that masters the 1500 commands of Blender and then MCP comes along and disrupts you. It's more like you need to be the person who can articulate precisely enough how you want something to be expressed visually so it conveys the experience to the customer that actually matters.
 And that really is important because you're going to have to explain it and then you're going to have to edit it and then you may have to polish it at the end. I've told you Stitch is better than prototyping. It's probably not polished all the way through. Remotion's polished clips can take a little while to run and their clips are not perfect.
 They don't do, as I said, super sophisticated animations. Blender MCP does a pretty good job with basic scenes, but if you're doing very complex organic modeling, it's not going to work well. These are all present limitations as of March 2026. I expect models to continue to get better and so on.
 But the thing to remember throughout is that we humans have an extraordinary ability to care for detail. It is going to be up to us to care for detail here so that we are actually able to express our taste and get what we want done out of our new superpowers. And and they are superpowers. For so many of us, including me, I am not a designer.
 This feels like I have Superman's cape. It's so cool. I can do so much. Enjoy those new superpowers. Don't be afraid to get into the MCP servers. Don't be afraid to get your first design.mmarkdown file. Don't be afraid to try out these tools. They will change your entire workflow if you do anything that touches any kind of design or visualization.
 And by the way, almost everybody needs front-end design. Almost everyone needs something beautifully made. And if you think, "Oh, I don't cuz I make dev tooling." I've got news for you. Part of the reason we choose dev tooling is because the websites are designed to be easy on the eye for developers. And that is a function of front-end design.
 Even if it looks like code, it's a function of front-end design. It's one of my pet passions. Ultimately, what this last several weeks of creative developments has freed us from is so much of the time we spent in creative tools that wasn't creative. It was operational, right? It was moving layers.
 It was adjusting key frames. It was exporting formats. It was rebuilding the same layout for the third darn time because the stakeholder changed one word in the headline. And the actual creative decisions were few and far between. that density of creative decisions can now go up. It's like this is the decision I want. Now go do it.
 One of the things I think is really interesting is that design is following development here. Developers knew for a long time that the command line was the right interface to get execution done at scale. Creative work arrived at the same insight. We are now at a point where you can generate, iterate, convert, and do all of the scheduling, executing work for design from the command line.
 And it doesn't matter if you were a developer before. As long as you have the ability to describe it, you could do it. This is the most widespread everybody can get in change that I've seen in AI in a while because design has always been gated. It's been gated by our ability to imagine and believe that we can actually move pixels around and create beautiful things.
 I think we've seen some just that over the last few years as Figma has started to come on the scene. It started to make some of these components easier to adjust. But this just blew the doors off. We are now in a place where anybody can do reasonably good prototyping design. And I think that frees us all to be so much more creative and focus on the value of decisioning.
And yes, I'll say it again, highquality designers are still needed to offer their great judgment and help us take these tools, these new paint brushes in the pallet box and actually use them to create incredible designs for customers, for ourselves, for our families, etc. Have fun. Use your new powers wisely.
Enjoy your Superman tips.
